---
format: pdf
---
# Project Charta
## Context and Scope
This project sits at the intersection of music analytics and data storytelling. The dataset is the publicly available Spotify Tracks Dataset from Kaggle, containing approximately 114,000 tracks spanning 114 genres. Each track is described by audio features computed by Spotify's audio analysis engine — including danceability, energy, valence, tempo, acousticness, loudness, and speechiness — along with metadata such as genre, artist, duration, key, and mode. A popularity score (0–100) derived from Spotify's internal algorithm serves as the primary variable of interest.
The project addresses the following topical aspects: the relationship between audio features and track popularity, how audio profiles differ across major genres, and what patterns in mood-related features like valence and energy reveal about mainstream success.
The central questions the project aims to answer are: Which audio features are most strongly associated with a track's popularity on Spotify? How do genres differ when described by their audio profiles? And what does a "popular" song actually sound like?
The expected benefit is a visually engaging, publicly accessible data story that gives music fans and curious readers a data-backed window into what makes songs resonate — presented in a way that requires no prior technical knowledge.
The visualization product will be published as a GitHub Page (Quarto-based digital article or web app), accessible online to lecturers, fellow students, and the general public. All code and data processing steps will be fully reproducible within the team's GitHub repository.

## Project Objectives and Success Criteria 
Objectives: 
- Enable users to understand which audio features (e.g. danceability, energy, valence) are associated with high popularity scores on Spotify.
- Allow users to compare the audio profiles of different music genres through intuitive visual summaries.
- Provide an engaging data story accessible to users without a data science background.
- Deliver a fully reproducible, well-documented analysis using Python within the course's GitHub and Quarto framework.

## Success Criteria

| Qualitative Objective | Quantitative Metric / Target |
|---|---|
| Clearly communicate key drivers of song popularity | At least 3 audio features analysed in relation to popularity, each with a dedicated visualization |
| Provide a genre-level audio comparison | At least 5 genres compared across at least 4 audio dimensions |
| Accessible to non-expert users | All charts include plain-language captions; no assumed data literacy |
| Fully reproducible analysis pipeline | Quarto project renders end-to-end without errors from the GitHub repository |
| Product published and accessible online | GitHub Page live at a public URL before the submission deadline |

Out of scope:
- No real-time data retrieval via the Spotify API — the analysis is based solely on the static Kaggle dataset snapshot.
- No predictive modelling or machine learning — the project focuses on exploration and visual communication, not prediction.
- No analysis of streaming counts, revenue, or artist career trajectories — only track-level audio features and popularity scores.
- No user personalisation or recommendation system features.


## Stakeholder Analysis
The project team consists of three students — Angelina, Cécile, and Charlotte — each with a defined role. Angelina acts as organiser, coordinating the team's workflow, deadlines, and communication. Cécile is responsible for visualisation, designing and implementing the charts and the overall visual language of the product. Charlotte leads the analysis, working through the dataset and deriving the insights that form the backbone of the data story. The three roles are interdependent: the analysis informs what needs to be visualised, and the organisation ensures both happen on time.
The course lecturers, Manuel Dömer and Wibke Weber, act as the primary clients and assessors. They define the requirements, provide feedback during coaching sessions, and grade the deliverables. Their expectations shape all major decisions — from the choice of visualisation forms to the structure of the documentation and the reproducibility of the code.
The audience of the published product consists primarily of fellow students, who will encounter the work during the oral presentation and may visit the GitHub Page. Secondarily, the product is intended for anyone with an interest in music — casual listeners or curious readers who stumble upon it online. This group has no active role in the project but shapes design decisions around accessibility and clarity.

## User Analysis

The visualization product targets two main user groups: fellow students from the same course context, and casual music fans who are daily Spotify listeners. Two personas are developed to represent these groups.

### Persona 1 — The Fellow Student

| Attribute | Description |
|---|---|
| Domain expertise | Moderate — familiar with Python, data analysis, and visual encoding principles |
| Data literacy | High — understands chart types, encodings, and design decisions |
| Technical environment | Laptop, standard screen size |
| Frequency of use | Once or twice — during the oral presentation or out of curiosity |

This persona is a student enrolled in the same data visualization course, working on their own project with a different dataset. They will encounter the product primarily during the oral presentation or by visiting the GitHub Page for inspiration or comparison.

**User tasks:** Functionally, they want to quickly grasp what the project is about and what insights were found, and may look at how other teams structured their data story or chose their visualizations. Socially, they might discuss the project with their own team or bring up findings in class. Emotionally, they want to feel engaged and inspired — not bored by something overly technical or dry.

**Pains:** They get frustrated if the product is hard to navigate or the narrative is unclear, since they have limited time and are juggling their own project. If the visualizations are too complex or unexplained, they will skim rather than read carefully. They are also quick to notice poor design choices, given their shared course background.

**Gains:** They appreciate a well-structured, visually clean product that gets to the point quickly. A creative visualization approach or an unexpected insight about music would make the experience memorable. A clearly reproducible and well-documented project also sets a useful reference point for their own work.

---

### Persona 2 — The Casual Music Fan

| Attribute | Description |
|---|---|
| Domain expertise | Low — no data science or music theory background |
| Data literacy | Basic — comfortable with simple charts; unfamiliar with technical terminology |
| Technical environment | Laptop, standard screen size |
| Frequency of use | One-time visitor, arriving via a shared link |

This persona is a daily Spotify listener who comes across the product through a shared link and engages with it out of pure curiosity about music. Age and gender are not defining characteristics — what matters is that music is a meaningful part of their daily life and they have a natural interest in understanding what makes certain songs resonate.

**User tasks:** Functionally, they want to explore an interesting question — why do some songs become popular? — in a way that feels effortless and enjoyable. Socially, if something surprises or delights them, they are likely to share it with friends. Emotionally, they want to feel like they learned something genuine about the music they listen to every day, without feeling talked down to or overwhelmed.

**Pains:** They will disengage quickly if the product feels academic, dry, or full of unexplained technical terms. If there is no clear story or hook within the first few seconds, they will not scroll further. They are not looking for a statistics lesson — they want the data to feel relevant to their own listening experience.

**Gains:** A surprising or counterintuitive insight — for example, that the most danceable songs are not always the most popular — would make the experience worthwhile. They leave satisfied if the product felt like a well-told story rather than a report, and if it gave them a new way to think about the music they already know.

## Situation Assessment
Describe the available resources (data, personnel, material, software/tools, infrastructure, etc.) and time as well as restrictions and constraints. Possible risks that may arise during the project are also identified.


## Visualization Concept
Translate the project objectives into a concrete visualization concept. This corresponds to the value map side of the Value Proposition Canvas [@Osterwalder2014] – describe how the proposed visualization product addresses the users' tasks, relieves their pains and creates gains. Address the following aspects:

* **Product form**: Composition of the visualization product – dashboard, data story, infographic, exploratory tool, etc.
* **Visual encodings**: Types of charts and visualizations to be used – e.g. bar chart, line chart, scatter plot, map, etc.
* **Interactivity**: Static vs. dynamic/interactive elements – filtering, drill-down, tooltips, linked views, etc.
* **Narrative and annotation**: Text elements, storytelling structure, annotations and contextual information.
* **Target medium and integration**: Embedding in the target environment – e.g. website, intranet, app, print, etc.

For each design decision, articulate the intended value along three dimensions:

* **Cognitive and analytical value**: How does the design amplify the user's ability to detect patterns, identify outliers, understand distributions or discover relationships [@card1999; @vanwijk2005]?
* **Communicative value**: How does the design support the communication of information to the target audience profile(s)? 
* **Experiential and aesthetic value**: How do the visual and interactive qualities of the design foster engagement, trust and willingness to adopt the product in practice?

Justify the concept by mapping it explicitly to the project objectives and the user needs identified in the user analysis.


## Project Plan
Divide the project into individual phases, describe them briefly and draw up a preliminary timetable, e.g. as a Gantt chart:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-project-plan
%%| fig-cap: Preliminary project plan in the form of a Gantt chart.
gantt
    title Project Plan
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    tickInterval 5day
    section Project Understanding
        Initiation workshop and context analysis     :a1, 2024-07-01, 1d
        Stakeholder and user analysis     :a3, 2024-07-02, 5d
        Situation assessment    :a4, 2024-07-06, 1d
        Project objectives and visualization concept    :a5, 2024-07-07, 1d
        Sign project charta: milestone, m1, 2024-07-08, 1d
    
    section Data Acquisition and Exploration
        Acquire data :a6, 2024-07-08, 3d
        Exploratory data analysis   :a7, 2024-07-09, 2d
        Discuss data report: milestone, m2, 2024-07-11, 1d
        
    section Visual Encoding and Design
        Overview charts   :a8, 2024-07-11, 3d
        Maps :a9, 2024-07-11, 5d
        Implement Dashboard prototype :a10, 2024-07-16, 5d
        Complete visualization report :a10, 2024-07-21, 1d
    section Evaluation
        Prepare presentation :a10, 2024-07-22, 2d
        Project presentation : milestone, m2, 2024-07-24, 4m
```

See [Mermaid syntax for Gantt charts](https://mermaid.js.org/syntax/gantt.html). It might not be displayed correctly in Safari &#8594; use Chrome. [Live editor with export functionality](https://mermaid.live/)

## Roles and Contact Details
List the people involved in the development work here with their role titles, tasks and contact details.
